content.full_text:
MICHAEL E. KYLE
ATTORNEY AT LAW
1171 MURRIETA BOULEVARD
LIVERMORE, CALIFORNIA 94550-4193

(415) 447-7020
FAX (415) 443-8730

April 25, 1990


California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Conflict of Interest Opinion, Livermore
    Area Recreation and Park District
    Gov. Code Section 83114(b); Title 2, Section 18329(b)

Gentlemen:

     I am writing on behalf of my client, the Livermore Area
Recreation and Park District (hereinafter the "District"). I am
writing at the direction and with the authorization of the Board
of Directors to raise a question whether one of the members of
our Board of Directors has a conflict of interest as defined by
the Act (Government Code Sections 81000-91015) and the
regulations adopted pursuant to that legislation.

Factual Background.

     The District is a special district formed pursuant to the
appropriate provisions of the Public Resources Code. The
District is governed by a five member elected Board of Directors.
The District employs approximately 50 full time staff and
approximately 125 part time staff. In general, the District is
responsible for the construction and maintenance of parks and
recreational facilities and for providing recreational activities
for the citizens of the Livermore area and others who utilize
our facilities. The District's operations are funded by the
property tax collected for it by the County of Alameda and to a
lesser extent, user fees and charges. Last year the District's
budget was approximately $10,000,000.00. The jurisdiction
covered by the District is approximately 250 square miles. The
number of inhabitants in the District is approximately 61,000
people. The City of Livermore (hereinafter the "City") lie
entirely within the geographical boundaries of the District
which extends into the unincorporated area of eastern Alameda county.

     Pursuant to the terms of a Joint Powers Agreement
(hereinafter the "JPA") executed in 1970, as amended, the City
and the District share responsibility for providing parks and
recreation services for the citizens of Livermore. A copy of the
Joint Powers Agreement is attached hereto, marked Exhibit "A",

A:\FPPCRE92.DOC

LARRD FPPC Opinion Request
April 25, 1990
Page 2

and incorporated herein by reference.  Generally, the City owns
most of the park sites within its boundaries, and the
responsibility for their development and maintenance is delegated
on a long term basis to the District. The District owns several
park sites in its own name including a 365 acre nature park
outside the City limits, but virtually all the neighborhood parks
are situated on lands owned by the City and operated and
maintained by the District.  The park sites are acquired by the
City through purchases, developer dedications and occasionally,
gifts.  Under the JPA, any funds collected by the City in the
form of developer in lieu fees are directly transferable to the
District for the construction of parks.

Mr. David Bing is a member of the Board of Directors of the
Livermore Area Recreation and Park District.  Mr. Bing's address
is 71 Trevarno Road, Livermore, CA, 94550, the District's office
and headquarters.  Mr. Bing is a principal stockholder in White
Oak landscape, a California corporation.  White Oak holds both a
general engineering contractor's license (an "A" license) and a
specialty contractor's license (a "C-27" license, landscaping).
For purposes of the contractor's licensing Board, the responsible
managing officer in the corporation is  Mr. Robert Quatman.  Mr.
Quatman is not otherwise involved in the issue(s) being raised in
this letter.

White Oak has two divisions: Construction and Maintenance.
White Oak does not now and has never directly contracted for
construction or maintenance work with the District.  One of the
types of projects which White Oak (as a subcontractor) can and
does submit bids for are "turn key" parks built by developers and
subsequently dedicated to the public entity having jurisdiction
for the development of the project and park in question.
Usually, title to the park is turned over to the public entity
through the dedication process.  The parks are always built by
White Oak in accordance with pre-drawn specifications.  White Oak
does not do any projects for public entities or developers on a
"design-build" basis.

The District would be the responsible lead agency in any
instance where a developer would be building and dedicating a
park within the city limits of Livermore.  The District, along
with the City's design review committee, engineering department,
planning staff and building inspection department would have
jurisdiction for actual approval and/or rejection of any
particular plan or project submitted and/or built by a developer.
Through custom and habit and pursuant to the terms of the JPA, it
is possible for District staff to have input in the process at
several stages.

First, as part of the tract map approval process, tentative
maps are delivered to the District for review and approval by the

A:\FPPCRO2.DOC

LARDD FPPC Opinion Request
April 25, 1990
Page 3

Board of Directors.  In the case of residential projects, the
Board usually recommends whether the City should require
dedication of park land or in the alternative, the payment of in
lieu fees.  The City of course is not bound by the District's
recommendation but generally pays deference to its position.
Secondly, where the construction of a park is called for,
District staff reviews the developer's plans and submittals for
conformance with the District's Master Plan and for the
appropriateness of the park location and the equipment being
proposed.  Finally, as the park is being built, District staff
works with City staff to inspect (for conformance to the plans
and specifications) and approve the developer's sub-contractor's
work.  In this regard, the City inspectors would inspect the
water and electrical portions of the job, and District staff
would inspect and approve (or reject) the installation of
playground equipment, irrigation equipment, etc.  In short, there
is a sharing of responsibility between the City and the District
staffs although the City has ultimate responsibility for ensuring
compliance with building codes and design criteria.

For purposes of addressing the issues sought to be raised in
this request, please assume that the only involvement of the
District Board would be rendering a decision on whether or not to
recommend construction of a park or receipt of in lieu fees under
the park dedication ordinance.  Assume further that at the time
such a decision is to be made by the board, Mr. Bing has no idea
of whether or not he will be submitting a bid to the developer
who is processing the tentative tract map through the City
approval process.  In other words, nothing is definite enough for
the developer at this juncture to have put out any part of the
project for bids.  Assume also that once the developer has
completed the approval process the construction of a park is
required and that White Oak submits a successful bid for
construction of the park.  Please also assume that the amount of
the bid is in excess of $100,000.00, and that Mr. Bing will be
compensated in excess of $250.00, for any work done on the
project.  Assume also that the involvement of District staff will
be as described above.

Questions Raised.

Given the facts stated above, does the Act require
boardmember Bing to refrain from submitting bids for the
construction of such a "turn key" park, and if the successful
bidder, from contracting with any developer who might propose a
project to be built within the geographical boundaries of the
District?  Further, under the Act, must boardmember Bing refrain
from participating in board deliberation and/or decision making
with respect to any such project and, if so, at what stage or
stages of the project?

A:\FPPCRE02.DOC

LARPD FPPC Opinion Request
April 25, 1990
Page 4

I trust that I have complied with your regulations and that
I have supplied enough background facts and information to allow
you to render an opinion under Government Code Section 8314(b),
and the regulation under Title 2, California Administrative Code,
Section 18329 (b).  If you note any deficiencies or require
additional information, immediate notification of the undersigned
is requested.  Thank you for your consideration.

Very Truly Yours,

Michael E. Kyle

MICHAEL E. KYLE

MEK/nl

cc: LARPD, Board of Directors


A:\FPPCREQ2.DOC

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 1, 1990


Michael E. Kyle
Attorney at Law
1171 Murrieta Boulevard
Livermore, CA  94550-4193

Re:  Letter No. 90-305

Dear Mr. Kyle:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on April 27, 1990 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission.  If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact Blanca Breeze an attorney in the Legal Division,
directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice.  If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to
information needed.  If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can.  (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

KED:plh


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

ATTEST:


William Payne
General Manager and ex-officio
clerk to the Board of Directors

DATED: ______________________     CITY OF LIVERMORE, a municipal
                                  corporation,

                                  By: __________________________
                                       "Mayor"        "City"


ATTEST:


_________________________________
CITY CLERK

APPROVED AS TO FORM:


_________________________________
CITY ATTORNEY


9


Law Offices of

Michael E. Kyle
(415) 734-8400
FAX (415) 734-8207

5000 Hopyard Road, Suite 400
Signature Center / East Lobby
Pleasanton, California 94588-3352

Of Counsel to:
Hallgrimson,
McNichols,
McCann & Inderbitzen

May 9, 1991

Ms. Blanca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street, Ste. 800
Sacramento, CA 95807-0807

RE: CONFLICT OF INTEREST OPINION
LIVERMORE AREA RECREATION AND PARK DISTRICT
YOUR FILE NO. I-90-305

Dear Ms. Breeze:

I am writing concerning the above captioned opinion issued
under your signature on July 3, 1990. My purpose in writing is
to request your cooperation in revising the specific code section
under which the opinion is issued.

Somehow, through my unartful use of the English language, I
did not make it clear to you in my request for the opinion, that
I was specifically acting on behalf of and had been personally
authorized by Mr. Bing to request the opinion on behalf of Mr.
Bing.

In other words, it was and always has been my intention to
make a request for an opinion under regulation section 18329(b),
Formal Written Advice. I would appreciate it if you could
reissue your opinion or simply issue an amendment to the existing
opinion indicating that it is a response being issued pursuant to
Government Code Section 83114(b).

I apologize that this request is so many months after the
issuance of your opinion in July, 1990. Though I noticed my
inadvertent and unartful use of the English language last summer,
I only recently discovered my failure to request this revision to
the opinion. Please call me if you have any questions or if I
may be of any assistance. Thank you for your anticipated
cooperation.

Very truly yours,

Michael E. Kyle

MEK/nl

cc: L.A.R.P.D.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a board member who is a principal stockholder in a landscaping contractor company must recuse himself from board decisions regarding park construction projects that his company might bid on, and whether his company is prohibited from submitting bids on such projects.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The document does not contain a formal FPPC conclusion/advice section; it is a request for advice dated April 25, 1990, accompanied by a May 1, 1990 acknowledgment letter and a May 9, 1991 follow-up letter requesting clarification of the code section under which the advice should be issued.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document contains the initial advice request with detailed factual background, an acknowledgment letter from FPPC, and a follow-up letter from the requesting attorney. The actual FPPC response/conclusion is not included in this document. Document appears to be part of a larger case file. The extraction of the 'question' field is verbatim from the 'Questions Raised' section of the letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Given the facts stated above, does the Act require boardmember Bing to refrain from submitting bids for the construction of such a 'turn key' park, and if the successful bidder, from contracting with any developer who might propose a project to be built within the geographical boundaries of the District? Further, under the Act must boardmember Bing refrain from participating in board deliberation and/or decision making with respect to any such project and, if so, at what stage or stages of the project?

CONCLUSION: The document does not contain a formal FPPC conclusion/advice section; it is a request for advice dated April 25, 1990, accompanied by a May 1, 1990 acknowledgment letter and a May 9, 1991 follow-up letter requesting clarification of the code section under which the advice should be issued.

embedding.summary: Michael E. Kyle requests FPPC advice on behalf of the Livermore Area Recreation and Park District regarding whether board member David Bing has a conflict of interest given his ownership stake in White Oak Landscape, which could bid on park construction projects that the board votes to recommend.
